---
title: "W15L1: Difference in Differences"
subtitle: "POLS 642 Intermediate Analysis of Political Data"
author: "Dr. Ches Thurber"
format: 
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    html-math-method: katex
execute: 
  echo: true
editor: visual
---

## Plan for Today

1\) "Bell Ringer"

2\) Event Studies: Segmented Regression

3\) Difference in Differences


## Packages

```{r}
#install.packages("ggdag")

library(ggplot2)
library(modelsummary)
library(dplyr)
library(fixest)
library(flextable)
#install.packages("causaldata")
library(causaldata)



```

## Bell-Ringer

I am studying the relationship between different types of regime transitions and democratization (measured with VDEM scores). My units of observation are country-years. How might I handle the time-dependent nature of democracy scores? (Many different correct answers)


## Types of Datasets

1\) Cross-sectional data: single observations from each unit (survey responses from a single wave)

2\) Time series data: multiple observations over time from a single unit (daily stock prices)

3\) Panel data: multiple observations over time from multiple unites (aka Time Series Cross-Sectional, TSCS)

## Strengths and Weaknesses

-   allows you to get more observations from your data (maybe)

-   track within group variation

-   But data from within groups will be highly correlated

-   FEs was one approach to dealing with this

# Segmented Regression (Event Study)

## What is it?

-   time-series (not panel data), where there is a "treatment" event at one point in time

-   does the relationship between time and outcome change after the event?

-   model with a simple interaction term:

    $$
    \hat{Y_i} = \beta_0 + \beta_1t + \beta_2After + \beta_3t*After + \epsilon
    $$

## Cardiac Study Event

![](https://theeffectbook.net/the-effect_files/figure-html/eventstudies-ambulances-1.png)

## The Concept of the Segmented Regression

-   Is the line different before versus after the event? In either intercept of slope!

-   You are comparing the post-treatment data to an assumed counterfactual that the trend before treatment would have continued if not for the treatment

-   But what if other things happened around that point in time that would have caused a change in trend even if the treatment had not occurred?

# Difference in Difference

## The Concept

-   when we have something akin to a treatment that is unlikely to be randomly applied and want to assess "before and after" effects, comparing treatment to nontreatment cases

-   in other words, we will compare not just before and after treatment in treated cases, but compare that difference to before and after the same point in time for untreated cases

-   e.g. What if we we had some districts that had implemented the Ambulance Quality reforms and others that did not?

## John Snow Example

| Region Supplier     | Death Rates in 1949 | Death Rates in 1854 |
|---------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Non-Lambeth (Dirty) | 134.9               | 146.6               |
| Lambeth (Clean)     | 130.1               | 84.9                |

: Death rates are deaths per 10,000 for the 1851 population from Snow (1855)

## How It's Done with Regression

-   create dummy variable for "TreatedGroup" (those units for which treatment was applied)

-   create a dummy variable for "AfterTreatment" (those units that came temporally after when the treatment was applied, even to the non-treatment groups)

-   create an interaction of TreatedGroup x AfterTreatment (this is what we'll be interested in)

-   use standard errors clustered at the group level

$$ 
\begin{aligned}
\hat{Y_i} = \beta_0 + \beta_1TreatedGroup + \beta_2AfterTreatment + \\
\beta_3TreatedGroup * AfterTreatment + \epsilon 
\end{aligned}$$

## Example: CA Organ Donation Policy Change

![](https://theeffectbook.net/the-effect_files/figure-html/differenceindifferences-rawdonations-1.png)

## In Action: Creating the Data

```{r}
od <- causaldata::organ_donations

od <- od %>%
     mutate(TreatedGroup = State == 'California',
            AfterTreatment = Quarter %in% c('Q32011','Q42011','Q12012'))

```

## In Action: Running the Regression

```{r}
Model1 <- lm(Rate ~ TreatedGroup * AfterTreatment, data = od)
msummary(Model1, vcov = ~State, stars = T, output = "flextable", gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared", "vcov.type")
) %>%
  flextable::autofit()
```

## Two-Way Fixed Effects Diff-in-Diff

```{r}
od <- od %>%
     mutate(Treated = State == 'California'& Quarter %in% c('Q32011','Q42011','Q12012'))
Model2 <- feols(Rate ~ Treated | State + Quarter, data = od)
msummary(list(Model1, Model2), vcov = ~State, stars = T, output = "flextable", gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared", "vcov.type")
) %>%
  flextable::autofit()
```

## Considerations

-   What if the treatment is applied at different points in time in different groups? (e.g. Maryland does the same thing, but in 2012, Vermont in 2013, etc...)

-   Parallel trends assumption

-   Additional covariates?

-   Literature on Diff in Diffs is getting updated daily with new problems to worry about...

## Conclusion

-   Diff in Diffs can be a powerful tool if you have panel data with some kind of treatment/intervention in some groups, but not others

-   But there are all kinds of assumptions and rapidly evolving debates on best practices

-   You should consider if your research/question and data are a good fit, BUT you will need to head into the weeds a little bit to figure out exactly how to implement
